title: Winter pig management with deep straw
subtitle: Keeping outdoor pigs warm, healthy, and fed through the cold months.
excerpt: Pigs in winter need more feed, more straw, and a dry place to sleep that stays dry. Outdoor pigs through a wet UK winter can be perfectly comfortable if the ark or shelter is correctly managed, but the work is heavier than summer, and the mud challenge is real. Here's the routine that keeps pigs healthy and bedding manageable from November through March.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, pig chapter. AHDB Pork, outdoor pig management guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A portable corrugated-iron or timber shelter, curved or triangular in section, used as housing for outdoor pigs.
A bedding system in which fresh straw is added regularly on top of existing bedding, allowing the base to compost and generate heat.

content.text: Pigs don't have a functional layer of body fat in the way sheep and cattle do, their main insulation is the straw they sleep in. A
packed with dry straw in a

system keeps pigs comfortable in temperatures well below freezing, provided the straw stays dry. The two things that undermine winter pig management are a leaking or flooded ark and insufficient straw.
Check the ark for leaks and repair them before the first frost. Corrugated iron arks rust along the fixing holes; check there with a screwdriver.
Move the ark onto the highest, driest ground available. Ground that drains well is infinitely better than level ground in winter.
Lay a thick base of straw, at least 30 cm. Pigs will compact this down within days; the compacted base ferments gently and generates heat from below.

Add a fresh armful or two of straw to the ark each day. Pigs use the same corner of the ark as a latrine; the other end stays clean. You don't need to muck out daily, just keep adding fresh straw to the sleeping end until the ark is too full. Full muck-outs every two to three weeks is a typical winter interval.

Increase concentrate rations by 10,15% from October onwards, pigs use more energy maintaining body temperature. Don't reduce back to summer rations until April. Watch the pigs' body condition weekly: ribs visible through a thin layer of fat is borderline; ribs not visible but easily felt means they're in good condition.

Pigs need water even in cold weather. Rubber drinkers crack in frost; a round rubber bucket or tub is more frost-resistant than an automatic drinker. Check twice a day and break ice if it forms. A pig that can't drink reduces feed intake immediately, water access and feed intake are directly linked.

Pigs on a wet field in winter will turn it into a mud bath within days. Rotating the ark onto fresh ground every two to three weeks prevents this but isn't always possible. Where rotation isn't an option, a gravel or hardcore pad around the ark entrance gives the pigs solid footing and reduces the amount of mud they carry back into the sleeping area.
